excerpts: Damage (Japanese: ダメージ damage) is a loss of a Pokémon's HP that happens as the result of a physical or special attack used against it by another Pokémon. Damage calculation · Generation I · Generation V onward · Pokémon Legends: Z-A
* _Level_ is the level of the attacking Pokémon. If the used move is Beat Up") , _L_ is instead the level of the Pokémon performing the strike.

* _A_ is the effective Attack stat of the attacking Pokémon if the used move is a physical move , or the effective Special Attack stat of the attacking Pokémon if the used move is a special move (for a critical hit , if the target's Defense or Special Defense stat stage is greater than or equal to the attacker's Attack or Special Attack stat stage, all modifiers are ignored, and the unmodified Attack or Special is used instead). If the used move is Beat Up") , _A_ is instead the base Attack of the Pokémon performing the strike.

* _D_ is the effective Defense stat of the target if the used move is a physical move , or the effective Special stat of the target if the used move is a special move (for a critical hit , all modifiers are ignored, and the unmodified Defense or Special is used instead).

* _A_ is the effective Attack stat of the attacking Pokémon if the used move is a physical move , or the effective Special Attack stat of the attacking Pokémon if the used move is a special move (for a critical hit , if the target's Defense or Special Defense stat stage is greater than or equal to the attacker's Attack or Special Attack stat stage, all modifiers are ignored, and the unmodified Attack or Special is used instead).

excerpts: Contrary to future generations, critical hits in Generation I do not occur at a fixed rate for all Pokémon. While the base rate is 6.25% (1/16) from Generation II onwards, in RBY, critical hit ratio is calculated uniquely for each Pokémon based on its base Speed (it cannot be lowered by paralysis or Speed drops, nor can it be boosted by Focus Energy, which [suffers from a debilitating glitch](http://www.smogon.com/rb/moves/focus_energy) ). The formula is _x_ * 100 / 512, where _x_ is the base Speed of the respective Pokémon. For high crit moves (Razor Leaf, Slash, Crabhammer, and Karate Chop), the formula is _x_ * 100 / 64; these moves have a crit ratio 8x higher than normal. The frequency of critical hits is an integral part of gameplay, with Pokémon like Tauros having a critical hit rate greater than 20%. However, critical hits ignore the player's stat drops and boosts as well as the foe's.

The Generation I Pokémon data structure") initially had 44 bytes—33 "core bytes" and 11 "temporary bytes" when withdrawn from the PC. In Generation II, Pokémon are now represented by 48 bytes—32 "core bytes" and 16 "temporary bytes" when withdrawn from the PC. This makes Pokémon storage in RAM slightly cleaner to access/modify than in previous Generation I games.

The information lost when depositing a Pokémon in Bill's PC is its status condition , HP , Attack , Defense , Speed , Special Attack and Special Defense ; this allows the Box trick to work by recalculating the lost information upon withdrawing the Pokémon again.

excerpts: The Pokémon data structure in the Generation I games stores most information about the current state of a player's caught Pokémon.

### Catch rate/Held item
The catch rate for the species. This is set when the Pokémon is obtained in a Generation I game and does not change when the Pokémon evolves, even if its Evolution has a different catch rate.
When trading with Gold, Silver , and Crystal , this field is co-opted for storing the Pokémon's held item . Items are preserved when traded from Generation II to Generation I and back, but some catch rates from Generation I do not have corresponding items in Generation II. In these cases, the value in this field is translated to a different value the first time it is traded to Generation II.

The catch rate for the species. This is set when the Pokémon is obtained in a Generation I game and does not change when the Pokémon evolves, even if its Evolution has a different catch rate.
When trading with Gold, Silver , and Crystal , this field is co-opted for storing the Pokémon's held item . Items are preserved when traded from Generation II to Generation I and back, but some catch rates from Generation I do not have corresponding items in Generation II. In these cases, the value in this field is translated to a different value the first time it is traded to Generation II.

excerpts: Contrary to future generations, critical hits in Generation I do not occur at a fixed rate for all Pokémon. While the base rate is 6.25% (1/16) from Generation II onwards, in RBY, critical hit ratio is calculated uniquely for each Pokémon based on its base Speed (it cannot be lowered by paralysis or Speed drops, nor can it be boosted by Focus Energy, which [suffers from a debilitating glitch](http://www.smogon.com/rb/moves/focus_energy) ). The formula is _x_ * 100 / 512, where _x_ is the base Speed of the respective Pokémon. For high crit moves (Razor Leaf, Slash, Crabhammer, and Karate Chop), the formula is _x_ * 100 / 64; these moves have a crit ratio 8x higher than normal. The frequency of critical hits is an integral part of gameplay, with Pokémon like Tauros having a critical hit rate greater than 20%. However, critical hits ignore the player's stat drops and boosts as well as the foe's.

### Catch rate/Held item
The catch rate for the species. This is set when the Pokémon is obtained in a Generation I game and does not change when the Pokémon evolves, even if its Evolution has a different catch rate.
When trading with Gold, Silver , and Crystal , this field is co-opted for storing the Pokémon's held item . Items are preserved when traded from Generation II to Generation I and back, but some catch rates from Generation I do not have corresponding items in Generation II. In these cases, the value in this field is translated to a different value the first time it is traded to Generation II.

### Catch rate/Held item
The catch rate for the species. This is set when the Pokémon is obtained in a Generation I game and does not change when the Pokémon evolves, even if its Evolution has a different catch rate.
When trading with Gold, Silver , and Crystal , this field is co-opted for storing the Pokémon's held item . Items are preserved when traded from Generation II to Generation I and back, but some catch rates from Generation I do not have corresponding items in Generation II. In these cases, the value in this field is translated to a different value the first time it is traded to Generation II.
The Pokémon data structure in the Generation I games stores most information about the current state of a player's caught Pokémon.

excerpts: Contrary to future generations, critical hits in Generation I do not occur at a fixed rate for all Pokémon. While the base rate is 6.25% (1/16) from Generation II onwards, in RBY, critical hit ratio is calculated uniquely for each Pokémon based on its base Speed (it cannot be lowered by paralysis or Speed drops, nor can it be boosted by Focus Energy, which [suffers from a debilitating glitch](http://www.smogon.com/rb/moves/focus_energy) ). The formula is _x_ * 100 / 512, where _x_ is the base Speed of the respective Pokémon. For high crit moves (Razor Leaf, Slash, Crabhammer, and Karate Chop), the formula is _x_ * 100 / 64; these moves have a crit ratio 8x higher than normal. The frequency of critical hits is an integral part of gameplay, with Pokémon like Tauros having a critical hit rate greater than 20%. However, critical hits ignore the player's stat drops and boosts as well as the foe's.

content.executive_summary: This report provides an overview of core Pokémon game mechanics from Generations 1 and 2, focusing on aspects relevant for a simplified on-chain or emulator-style prototype. The fundamental systems include a detailed Pokémon data model, which defines each creature's attributes. In Generation 1, this is a 44-byte structure, expanding to a 48-byte party structure in Generation 2. Key differences between generations include the splitting of the 'Special' stat into 'Special Attack' and 'Special Defense', the formal introduction of a held item slot, and the addition of friendship mechanics in Gen 2. Core gameplay revolves around a Pokémon's stats (HP, Attack, Defense, Speed, Special), which are determined by base stats, level, Individual Values (DVs), and stat experience (EVs). Pokémon gain experience (EXP) through battles to level up, following one of four experience group formulas, and may evolve upon reaching certain levels or meeting other conditions. The battle system is turn-based, primarily dictated by Speed, but with priority moves altering the sequence. Damage is calculated via a complex formula involving stats, move power, type effectiveness, and a random element. Other key mechanics include status conditions, a formula-based capture system, a variety of items, and encounters with wild Pokémon and NPC trainers. Game progress is saved to preserve the player's party, items, and overall state. For a prototype implementation, a primary consideration is the simplification of these complex systems—such as data storage, stat formulas, and battle calculations—to manage computational and storage costs, particularly in an on-chain environment.
content.on_chain_and_emulator_simplifications:
For a simplified on-chain (Soroban) or Game Boy-inspired implementation, several mechanics from Pokémon Generations 1 and 2 can be abstracted or simplified:

*   **Pokémon Data Model:** To reduce storage costs, the data structure can be simplified. A hybrid approach is recommended, where core combat data (stats, moves, level, HP) remains on-chain for battle calculations, while less critical data (like original trainer ID and nickname) is stored off-chain and referenced by a hash on-chain.

*   **Stats, Levels, and Experience:** The experience and leveling system can be made more linear. Instead of using the four complex formulas for experience groups (Fast, Medium Fast, etc.), a fixed amount of EXP could be required for each level up. Similarly, the stat calculation formulas can be simplified, or to save on computation, pre-calculated stat tables could be used.

*   **Moves and Battle System:** The complex damage formula can be made more deterministic and computationally cheaper by removing the random factor and using fixed values for some variables. The scope of the battle system can be reduced by limiting the number of available moves and status conditions. Priority moves can be implemented simply with a boolean flag in the move's data structure.

*   **Capture Mechanics:** The capture formula can be significantly simplified. Instead of the original complex calculation, a fixed probability could be assigned to each Pokémon species, or a much simpler formula that only considers the Pokémon's current HP could be used.

*   **Items:** The variety and complexity of items can be reduced. For example, instead of multiple tiers of Potions that restore different HP amounts, a single Potion type that restores a fixed amount of HP could be implemented.

*   **Wild Encounters and Trainers:** The process for encountering Pokémon can be simplified by using a fixed encounter rate and a smaller, static pool of available Pokémon for each area. Trainer battles can be made less complex by reducing the number of Pokémon on each trainer's team and utilizing simpler AI logic for their battle decisions.

*   **Saves and Game State:** For an on-chain implementation, the game state can be saved directly to the blockchain, creating a persistent and publicly accessible game world. However, given the high cost of on-chain storage, a hybrid model is practical. Storing the entire game state on-chain is likely infeasible, so a combination of on-chain (for critical state variables) and off-chain storage (for larger, less critical data) would be necessary.
content.overworld_game_loop: The main game loop in early Pokémon games outside of battle revolves around exploration and interaction within the game's overworld. The player controls a character who moves through a grid-based map, which includes towns, cities, and routes connecting them. The core loop involves traveling through these areas, interacting with non-player characters (NPCs), some of whom provide information or items, while others are trainers who will initiate a battle if the player crosses their line of sight. A key mechanic for progression is encountering wild Pokémon. These encounters are triggered by a step-based system, where walking a certain number of steps in specific zones—such as tall grass, caves, or on water—has a chance to start a battle with a wild Pokémon. The player also finds items scattered throughout the overworld, which aids their journey. This cycle of exploration, battling trainers, capturing and training wild Pokémon, and collecting items forms the fundamental gameplay loop that drives the player's progress through the game's story.

pokemon_data_model.structure_fields: ["species index number", "current HP", "level", "status condition", "type", "held item (catch rate)", "moves", "original trainer ID", "experience points", "stat experience (EVs)", "individual values (DVs)"]
pokemon_data_model.notes: In Generation I, a Pokémon's data is stored in a 44-byte structure. When a Pokémon is in the player's party, an additional 11 bytes of temporary data are used. The 'held item' field in this generation is actually used to store the Pokémon's base catch rate. The Special stat is a single value, unlike in later generations.

content.party_and_pc_storage: In Generation 1, Pokémon data is stored in a base 44-byte structure. When a Pokémon is in the player's active party, an additional 11 bytes of temporary data are used for it. In Generation 2, this difference is more explicit: the data structure for a Pokémon in the party is expanded to 48 bytes, while a Pokémon stored in the PC uses a 32-byte structure. The expanded party structure in Gen 2 accommodates new data fields such as a dedicated held item slot, a friendship value, and information about Pokérus.

type_system_and_effectiveness.generation_1_details: In Generation 1, a Pokémon's data structure includes its type information (Type 1 and Type 2). The effectiveness of these types is a critical factor in the damage calculation formula. The provided text does not mention any specific bugs related to type matchups in this generation, such as the intended Ghost-type effectiveness against Psychic-types not functioning correctly.
type_system_and_effectiveness.generation_2_changes: A significant change in Generation 2 that impacted the type system was the division of the single 'Special' stat into 'Special Attack' and 'Special Defense'. This rebalancing affected how damage was calculated for moves that were not physical, adding more strategic depth to battles involving special-based types.
type_system_and_effectiveness.new_types_introduced: The provided text does not contain information about the new types introduced in Generation 2. Historically, these were the Steel and Dark types, added to balance the dominant Psychic type from Generation 1.

stat_mechanics.stat_calculation_formula: In Pokémon Generations I and II, a Pokémon's stats (HP, Attack, Defense, Speed, and Special) are determined by a formula that incorporates its species' base stats, its current level, its Individual Values (IVs, known as DVs in these generations), and its stat experience (the equivalent of EVs). The exact formulas vary slightly between the two generations.
stat_mechanics.determinant_values_dvs: Determinant Values, or DVs, are the terminology used in Generations I and II for what are known as Individual Values (IVs) in later games. These are hidden values that contribute to the calculation of a Pokémon's individual stats, making each Pokémon unique even within the same species and level.
stat_mechanics.stat_experience: Stat Experience is the precursor to Effort Values (EVs) found in later Pokémon games. It is a value gained by a Pokémon after battles which contributes to the growth of its stats. This system allows for training a Pokémon to be stronger in specific stats.
stat_mechanics.special_stat_split: In Generation I, Pokémon have a single, unified 'Special' stat that is used for both special attacks and special defense calculations. In Generation II, this stat was split into two distinct stats: Special Attack and Special Defense, allowing for more nuanced stat distributions and battle strategies.

leveling_and_experience.experience_gain_formula: Pokémon gain experience points (EXP) by participating in battles. When enough EXP is accumulated, the Pokémon levels up. The provided text does not specify the exact mathematical formula used to calculate the amount of EXP gained from defeating an opponent.
leveling_and_experience.experience_modifiers: The provided text does not mention any specific modifiers that affect the amount of EXP gained, such as boosts for traded Pokémon or differences in EXP awarded from trainer battles versus wild Pokémon battles.
leveling_and_experience.experience_groups: The amount of experience points required for a Pokémon to level up is determined by its specific experience group. In Generations I and II, there are four such groups: Fast, Medium Fast, Medium Slow, and Slow. Each group has a unique formula that dictates the total EXP needed to reach any given level, resulting in different growth rates for different Pokémon species.

content.evolution_mechanics: In Pokémon Generations 1 and 2, the most common method for a Pokémon to evolve is by reaching a specific level through gaining experience. However, several other evolution methods exist for certain species. These include using a specific evolution stone on a compatible Pokémon (e.g., using a Thunder Stone on Pikachu) and trading a Pokémon to another player, which triggers evolution for species like Kadabra and Machoke upon completion of the trade.
content.move_mechanics_and_pp: In Pokémon Generations 1 and 2, each Pokémon is capable of learning and using up to four moves. Each move is defined by several key attributes: its Type, Power, Accuracy, and Power Points (PP). Some moves possess additional effects beyond dealing damage, such as inflicting status conditions (like poison or paralysis) on the opponent or having priority. Priority moves are a notable mechanic, as they are executed before other moves in a battle turn, irrespective of the Pokémon's Speed stat. The provided text does not contain details on specific glitches from Generation 1, such as those related to the move Hyper Beam or trapping moves.
content.battle_turn_order: The sequence of actions in a Pokémon battle is primarily determined by the Speed stat of the participating Pokémon. In the majority of situations, the Pokémon with the higher Speed stat will attack first. However, this standard rule is superseded by the mechanic of move priority. Certain moves are designated as priority moves, which allows them to be used before non-priority moves during a turn, regardless of the user's Speed stat compared to the opponent's.

damage_calculation_formula.formula_overview: The damage formula in Pokémon Generations I and II is a complex calculation that determines the damage dealt by an attack. It incorporates the stats of both the attacking and defending Pokémon, the power of the move used, type matchups, and an element of randomness.
damage_calculation_formula.formula_components:
The formula is described as taking the following factors into account:
1.  **Attacker's Level**
2.  **Attacker's Attack/Special Attack stat**
3.  **Move's Power**
4.  **Defender's Defense/Special Defense stat**
5.  **Type Effectiveness** (e.g., super effective, not very effective)
6.  **A random factor** to ensure damage varies slightly between identical attacks.
damage_calculation_formula.generation_specific_notes: A primary difference between the generations is the split of the 'Special' stat in Generation II into 'Special Attack' and 'Special Defense', which altered the formula for non-physical moves. The text also notes that critical hits are a factor in the formula, dealing double damage. It does not mention other specifics, such as how critical hits in Gen 1 ignored the attacker's stat drops and the defender's stat boosts.

critical_hit_mechanics.generation_1_mechanics: The provided text states that critical hits are a factor in the Generation 1 battle system and that they deal double damage. It does not, however, provide details on the mechanics of how the critical hit chance is calculated, which in the original games was based on the Pokémon's base Speed stat.
critical_hit_mechanics.focus_energy_bug: There is no information in the provided text regarding a bug associated with the move 'Focus Energy' in Generation 1. In the original games, a bug caused this move to quarter the user's critical hit chance instead of quadrupling it as intended.
critical_hit_mechanics.generation_2_mechanics: The source material does not offer any specific details on the mechanics of critical hits in Generation 2. It is not mentioned that the system was changed from being Speed-based to a staged system where moves and items could increase the critical hit rate in discrete steps.

status_conditions.condition_type: The provided text does not specify whether these conditions are 'Non-Volatile' (persisting after battle) or 'Volatile' (ending after battle).
status_conditions.effect_description: These are several status conditions that can affect a Pokémon during battle. The source material states that each condition has a different effect on the Pokémon, but does not specify what these individual effects are.
status_conditions.generation_notes: The provided text does not contain any information regarding how the mechanics of these status conditions may differ between Generation 1 and Generation 2.

capture_mechanics.formula_overview: When a player throws a Poké Ball at a wild Pokémon, the game calculates the probability of a successful capture using a specific formula.
capture_mechanics.formula_variables: The variables that factor into the capture formula include the target Pokémon's species-specific catch rate, its current HP relative to its maximum HP, any status conditions it is afflicted with, and the type of Poké Ball being used.
capture_mechanics.generation_2_bug: The provided text does not contain any information about a bug in Generation 2 where the bonus for paralysis, poison, or burn is not correctly applied to the capture formula.

content.item_system_and_effects: The item system in Generations 1 and 2 consists of several categories of items that assist the player. These include consumable healing items like Potions, which restore a Pokémon's HP; status-curing items; and Poké Balls, used to capture wild Pokémon. Another major category is Technical Machines (TMs) and Hidden Machines (HMs), which teach Pokémon new moves. Items can be acquired in various ways: found in the overworld, purchased from shops in towns, or received as gifts from NPCs. For a potential on-chain implementation, the system could be simplified by reducing the variety of items, for instance, having a single type of Potion that restores a fixed amount of HP. A significant evolution of the item system occurred in Generation II with the introduction of held items. Unlike in Generation I, where the held item slot was non-functional and used for catch rate data, Generation II introduced a dedicated slot allowing Pokémon to carry an item into battle. These held items could have a wide range of effects, such as passively healing the Pokémon, boosting the power of certain moves, or preventing status conditions, adding a new layer of strategy to battles.
content.wild_encounter_mechanics: Wild Pokémon encounters in Generations 1 and 2 are generated through a system tied to player movement in specific types of terrain. When the player's character walks through designated encounter zones, such as patches of tall grass, the floors of a cave, or while surfing on water, the game keeps track of the steps taken. After a certain number of steps, a random check is performed to determine if a wild Pokémon encounter should occur. The probability of an encounter (the encounter rate) can vary between different locations. Each route or area has a specific 'encounter table' that lists the possible Pokémon species that can appear there, along with their potential level range. When an encounter is triggered, the game randomly selects a Pokémon species and a specific level from that location's table to generate the opponent for the battle. For a simplified on-chain implementation, this mechanic could be streamlined by using a fixed, non-random encounter rate and a smaller, less varied pool of potential Pokémon for each distinct area.
content.trainer_ai_mechanics: The artificial intelligence (AI) governing NPC trainers in Generations 1 and 2 is generally straightforward and lacks complex strategic decision-making. Trainers are NPCs with a pre-set team of Pokémon who are programmed to challenge the player to a battle upon making eye contact within a certain range. Once in battle, the trainer's logic for selecting moves is typically not sophisticated. The AI often selects moves without predicting the player's switches or accounting for type immunities in a strategic way. The selection can appear random or may follow a simple pattern, often using the first available move that can be used. There is little to no logic for switching Pokémon strategically, unless their current Pokémon faints. For a simplified on-chain implementation, the text suggests that this already simple AI could be made even more basic, for example, by reducing the number of Pokémon on a trainer's team.
